konular.ozet: Service members and veterans who have served honorably may apply for U.S. citizenship using Form N-400, Application for Naturalization, together with Form N-426 if currently serving, or official discharge documentation such as a DD-214. The filing fee is waived for currently serving members and eligible veterans. Many military installations have a designated USCIS liaison who can assist with the application process.

Military Parole in Place (MilPIP) allows USCIS to grant temporary status, on a case-by-case basis, to the spouses, widow(er)s, parents, and children of active-duty service members, Selected Reserve members, and veterans who were not dishonorably discharged — enabling them to remain in the U.S. and potentially apply to adjust their immigration status. To apply, submit Form I-131 (selecting Part 1, Item 8) with supporting documents; as of July 21, 2025, all MilPIP applications must be filed at the USCIS facility in Montclair, CA rather than local field offices. Parole is granted in one-year increments and does not by itself confer permanent resident status.

Military basic pay is the primary compensation for service members, calculated based on rank and cumulative years of service and paid twice monthly; the same pay tables apply uniformly across all branches. Annual increases are tied to the U.S. Bureau of Labor's Employment Cost Index and require Congressional approval. Basic pay is generally subject to federal income tax, with exceptions for members serving in designated combat zones.

Basic Allowance for Housing (BAH) is an additional monthly pay to help service members cover civilian housing costs when government quarters are not provided, with rates determined by rank, geographic duty station, and dependent status. BAH is intended to offset local civilian rental market costs and is recalculated annually. Current rates can be looked up using the BAH Rate Lookup tool provided by the Defense Travel Management Office.

Basic Allowance for Subsistence (BAS) is a monthly allowance provided to enlisted members and officers to help offset the cost of food while on active duty. Enlisted members and officers receive different BAS rates, which are updated annually and published by the Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS). Current BAS rates are available in the pay tables section of the DFAS website.

The Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) is a retirement savings and investment plan for uniformed services members — including active duty, National Guard, and Reserve — that functions similarly to a civilian 401(k). Service members covered by the Blended Retirement System (those who joined on or after January 1, 2018) receive automatic government contributions of 1% of basic pay, plus matching contributions up to 4% after two years of service. Contributions are made through the member's branch pay system, and accounts may be rolled over to a civilian retirement plan or IRA when leaving the service.

TRICARE provides health care coverage to active-duty service members, military retirees, National Guard and Reserve members, and their family members who are enrolled in the Defense Enrollment Eligibility Reporting System (DEERS), as well as eligible survivors and Medal of Honor recipients. Eligibility is determined by the service member's branch of service and reported through DEERS. Available plans and coverage options vary by beneficiary category and geographic location; use the TRICARE Plan Finder to identify plans available in your area.

TRICARE For Life is a Medicare-wraparound benefit available to TRICARE-eligible beneficiaries who have both Medicare Part A and Part B, regardless of age or where they live worldwide. When receiving care in the U.S. or U.S. territories, Medicare pays first and TRICARE covers most of the remaining costs, generally resulting in no out-of-pocket expenses for covered services. Enrollment in TFL is automatic when Medicare Part A and Part B take effect; Medicare Part B premiums still apply.

Veterans may be eligible for VA health care covering primary and specialty care, mental health services, prescriptions, home health, and more; income may affect eligibility and out-of-pocket costs. Eligible family members — including spouses, surviving spouses, dependent children, and caregivers — may also qualify for certain benefits. Applications can be submitted online, by phone, by mail, or in person; the VA health benefits hotline is 877-222-8387.

CHAMPVA (Civilian Health and Medical Program of the Department of Veterans Affairs) is a cost-sharing health care program for eligible spouses, dependent children, and surviving spouses of veterans who are permanently and totally disabled due to a service-connected condition, or who died from a service-connected disability, provided they do not qualify for TRICARE. Covered services include inpatient and outpatient care, mental health services, prescriptions, and preventive care. To apply, submit VA Form 10-10d with supporting documents online, by mail, or by fax to the VA.

The TRICARE Dental Program (TDP) is a voluntary dental insurance plan administered by United Concordia, available to eligible family members of service members who are registered in DEERS. To enroll, the military sponsor must have at least 12 months of remaining service at the time of enrollment; enrollment is completed through milConnect. Active duty service members receive dental care through the military dental system rather than the TDP.

Military OneSource provides free, confidential counseling and mental health support to service members, veterans, and their families — including individual, couple, and family counseling, the Military and Family Life Counseling program, and peer-to-peer support. Under the Brandon Act, service members may request a mental health evaluation at any time by notifying their chain of command. Support is available 24/7 at 800-342-9647, by secure online chat, or by dialing 988 and pressing 1 for the Veterans Crisis Line.

The GI Bill provides education and training benefits — including coverage for tuition, fees, books, and a monthly housing stipend — to eligible veterans, service members, and in some cases their dependents. Major programs include the Post-9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33) for those who served after September 10, 2001, the Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty (Chapter 30), and the Montgomery GI Bill Selected Reserve (Chapter 1606) for Reserve and Guard members. The VA website provides a comparison tool to help eligible individuals determine which benefit applies to their situation.

Service members may transfer unused Post-9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33) education benefits to a spouse or dependent children by completing at least six years of service and agreeing to serve four additional years. Eligible dependents enrolled in DEERS can receive up to 36 months of education benefits, which may be used for tuition, housing stipend, and other education-related costs. Requests to transfer benefits must be submitted through milConnect while the service member is still on active duty or in the Selected Reserve.

Eligible family members of service members, retirees, and certain veterans who are registered in the Defense Enrollment Eligibility Reporting System (DEERS) may obtain a military dependent ID card granting access to TRICARE health coverage, commissary and exchange shopping, Morale Welfare and Recreation programs, and other installation services. Applications require DD Form 1172-2 signed by the military sponsor, two forms of identification, and proof of relationship; first-time applicants must be enrolled in DEERS before applying. Applications can be submitted online through the ID Card Office Online portal or in person at a RAPIDS site.

The Military Spouses Residency Relief Act (MSRRA) allows military spouses to maintain their legal state of residency when relocating with a service member on PCS orders, preventing double taxation and preserving state-based voting rights and benefits. A 2022 amendment to the Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) also grants military spouses the right to use a professional license or certificate from their home state at a similar scope of practice in a new state when the family moves due to military orders. The service member's branch may also reimburse spouses up to $1,000 for costs related to transferring a professional license across state lines.

Department of Defense Education Activity (DoDEA) schools provide tuition-free K–12 education to dependents of active-duty service members living on or in the vicinity of military installations worldwide. A 2025 law expanded eligibility to include dependents of full-time active-duty members residing off the installation, subject to available space. Enrollment requires documentation confirming dependent status in DEERS and assignment orders to the installation.

The SCRA protects active-duty service members, National Guard and Reserve members called to active duty, and their families by capping interest rates on pre-service debts at 6%, allowing lease termination without penalty for qualifying PCS moves or deployments of 90 days or more, and protecting against mortgage foreclosure during and up to nine months after active-duty service. Additional protections cover eviction, civil court proceedings, automobile and phone service leases, and professional license portability for service members and their spouses. Free legal assistance is available through installation legal offices; contact Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 for a referral.

Service members renting off-post should confirm that their lease includes a military clause permitting early termination for a PCS move or deployment; the SCRA independently grants this right for qualifying service members with proper written notice. The Fair Housing Act prohibits discrimination based on protected characteristics when renting or buying a home. For questions about a specific lease or tenant rights, free legal assistance is available through your installation's JAG (Judge Advocate General) office.

The VA-backed home loan program allows eligible veterans, service members, and surviving spouses to purchase, build, or refinance a home — in many cases with no down payment required. To use the benefit, applicants must first obtain a Certificate of Eligibility (COE), which can be requested online, by mail, or through a lender. Standard credit and income requirements still apply through the private lender.

VA disability compensation provides a monthly tax-free payment to veterans whose illness, injury, or mental health condition — including PTSD — was caused or made worse by their military service; surviving spouses, dependent children, and parents may also qualify. Payment amounts are based on the disability rating assigned to the veteran and the number of dependents; current rate tables are available on the VA website. Claims can be filed online, by phone, mail, or fax, and a Veterans Service Organization (VSO) representative can assist at no cost; call 800-827-1000 for help.

The VA Pension program provides financial benefits to eligible wartime veterans who have limited income and are no longer able to work due to a non-service-connected disability or are of qualifying age, and to certain surviving spouses and children of wartime veterans. Veterans who need help with daily activities or who are substantially confined may be eligible for additional Aid and Attendance or Housebound pension benefits. Applications can be filed at VA.gov or with help from an accredited VSO; call 800-827-1000 for assistance.

The VA Vocational Rehabilitation and Employment program helps service members and veterans with service-connected disabilities that limit their ability to work prepare for, find, and maintain suitable employment. The program offers multiple tracks: reemployment assistance, rapid access to employment, self-employment support, long-term services, and independent living. Eligible individuals can apply online at VA.gov using VA Form 28-1900.

The Veterans ID Card (VIC) is a digital photo ID that serves as proof of military service and allows veterans to receive discounts offered to veterans at participating businesses nationwide. Eligibility requires active duty service ending in an honorable or general discharge; National Guard and Reserve members with qualifying service may also apply. Applications are submitted online at va.gov using a government-issued ID, discharge documentation, and a compliant photo.

The Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers (PCAFC) provides support to caregivers of eligible veterans who have a combined service-connected disability rating of 70% or more and need at least six months of in-person personal care assistance. Eligible Primary Family Caregivers may receive a monthly financial stipend, CHAMPVA health coverage if otherwise uninsured, mental health counseling, and at least 30 days of respite care per year. Applications are submitted online or in person through VA's Caregiver Support Program at caregiver.va.gov.

The VA National Cemetery Administration provides eligible veterans with burial in a VA national cemetery at no cost, including a gravesite, opening and closing of the grave, perpetual care, and a government-furnished headstone or marker. Additional no-cost benefits include a burial flag and a Presidential Memorial Certificate; a monetary burial allowance may also be available to help offset funeral expenses. Eligible spouses and dependent children of veterans may also be interred in a VA national cemetery.

Service members serving in a designated combat zone can exclude their basic pay, reenlistment bonuses, and imminent danger pay from federal income tax for each month of qualifying service. The exclusion is applied automatically — the military organization certifies eligibility and excludes the income from your W-2. Social Security and Medicare taxes still apply to excluded combat pay.

Hostile Fire Pay (HFP) and Imminent Danger Pay (IDP) are special pays of $225 per month for service members subjected to hostile fire or mine explosions, or for those on duty in areas officially designated as imminent danger zones — members receive one or the other, not both. HFP eligibility is certified at the lowest level of command; service members hospitalized for combat-related wounds may continue to receive the pay for up to 12 months of treatment. Both types of special pay are excluded from federal income tax when earned in a designated combat zone.

Active-duty service members with dependents who are required to be away from their permanent duty station for more than 30 consecutive days due to military orders — such as deployments, unaccompanied overseas assignments, or shipboard duty — receive a Family Separation Allowance of $300 per month (effective January 1, 2026). FSA is initiated by submitting DD Form 1561 to the installation's personnel support office. Both spouses receive FSA if both are service members involuntarily separated from their dependents.

TRICARE Reserve Select (TRS) is a premium-based health plan available to qualified Selected Reserve members and their families who are not on active duty orders for more than 30 days and are not eligible for the Federal Employees Health Benefits program. The sponsor must enroll first; family members can then purchase coverage separately through the regional TRICARE contractor. TRS provides coverage similar to TRICARE Select, including access to any TRICARE-authorized provider.

TRICARE Young Adult (TYA) is a purchasable health plan for unmarried adult children of eligible sponsors who have aged out of standard TRICARE coverage, typically at age 21 (or 23 if a full-time college student), and are under age 26. TYA is available as TYA-Prime (assigned primary care manager) or TYA-Select (any authorized provider) and requires a monthly premium. The dependent must not be eligible for employer-sponsored health coverage to qualify.

The Veterans Crisis Line provides free, confidential 24/7 crisis support for veterans, active-duty service members, National Guard and Reserve members, and their families and friends — no VA enrollment or benefits eligibility is required. Reach trained responders by calling 988 then pressing 1, texting 838255, or chatting online at VeteransCrisisLine.net. Responders listen, provide immediate support, and connect callers with local resources.

Servicemembers' Group Life Insurance (SGLI) is a low-cost term life insurance program that automatically covers eligible service members for up to $500,000 in $50,000 increments, at a cost of just 5 cents per $1,000 of coverage per month. Eligible members include active-duty military, Ready Reserve and National Guard members with at least 12 scheduled training periods per year, commissioned NOAA and USPHS officers, and ROTC members. Full coverage is automatic at enrollment; members must actively elect to reduce or decline it.

The Yellow Ribbon Program supplements the Post-9/11 GI Bill to help cover tuition and fee costs at private, out-of-state, foreign, or graduate schools that exceed the standard GI Bill cap. Eligibility requires qualifying for Post-9/11 GI Bill benefits at the 100% level and meeting service criteria such as 36 months of active duty. Participating schools contribute funds toward the remaining cost gap, and the VA matches those contributions dollar for dollar.

Active-duty service members, their dependents, and certain reserve component members are entitled to free legal assistance through military Judge Advocate General (JAG) offices on or near installations. Services commonly include help with wills, powers of attorney, landlord-tenant disputes, consumer protection issues, and deployment-related legal matters. Use the Armed Forces Legal Assistance locator at legalassistance.law.af.mil to find the nearest office by state or zip code.

The VA offers two grants to help veterans with service-connected disabilities buy, build, or modify a permanent home: the Specially Adapted Housing (SAH) grant (up to $126,526 for FY 2026) and the Special Home Adaptation (SHA) grant (up to $25,350 for FY 2026). Eligibility requires specific service-connected disabilities such as loss of use of more than one limb, blindness in both eyes, or certain burn injuries. Each grant may be used up to six times over a veteran's lifetime.

konular.baslik: HUD-VASH — Housing Vouchers for Homeless Veterans
konular.ozet: HUD-VASH is a joint program between the Department of Housing and Urban Development and the VA that combines Housing Choice Voucher rental assistance with VA case management and supportive services. The program helps homeless veterans and their families secure permanent housing while connecting them to healthcare, mental health treatment, and other VA support. Since 2008, HUD has distributed over 116,000 vouchers across all 50 states, Guam, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands.

The Sergeant First Class Heath Robinson Honoring our PACT Act of 2022 is the largest expansion of VA health care and benefits in history, covering veterans exposed to burn pits, Agent Orange, radiation, and other toxic substances. The law adds over 20 new presumptive conditions — including cancers and respiratory illnesses for Gulf War and post-9/11 veterans — eliminating the requirement to prove that military service caused the disease. It also broadens VA health care enrollment eligibility and extends benefits to survivors of deceased veterans who qualify under the new conditions.

The Survivor Benefit Plan (SBP) allows retiring service members to elect coverage that provides surviving dependents with a lifetime monthly annuity based on a percentage of the retiree's covered retired pay, adjusted annually for inflation. Full spouse and child coverage is automatic at retirement unless the retiree actively declines it; spouse coverage cannot be reduced without written spousal consent. Premiums are deducted from gross retired pay before taxes, and beneficiaries report the retiree's death to DFAS at 800-321-1080 to begin receiving payments.

The Survivors' and Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA) program provides monthly education payments to the children and spouses of veterans who died in service, are missing in action, or have a permanent and total service-connected disability. Benefits cover undergraduate and graduate degrees, vocational training, apprenticeships, and certification programs, typically for 36 to 45 months depending on when training began. Eligible dependents apply through the VA and receive monthly payments to help cover tuition and living expenses.

TRICARE Prime Remote is a managed care option for active-duty service members and their families living more than 50 miles or a one-hour drive from a military hospital or clinic. Eligible participants include active-duty personnel, activated National Guard and Reserve members on qualifying orders, and their enrolled family members in designated remote areas. TPR eliminates out-of-pocket costs for referred care, offers travel reimbursement, and requires enrollment through a regional TRICARE contractor.

The Transitional Assistance Management Program (TAMP) provides 180 days of premium-free TRICARE health coverage to service members involuntarily separating from active duty under honorable conditions and to National Guard and Reserve members separating after 30 or more consecutive days of qualifying active duty. During the 180-day period, beneficiaries may access TRICARE Prime, Select, or other available plan options. TAMP is designed to bridge the gap between active-duty TRICARE and civilian or other health insurance.

TRICARE provides four prescription fill options: military pharmacies (typically no copay), home delivery through the TRICARE Mail Order Pharmacy administered by Express Scripts (cost-effective for maintenance medications), retail network pharmacies, and non-network pharmacies. Beneficiaries can manage prescriptions, request refills, and check coverage online or through the Express Scripts mobile app. Copay amounts depend on your beneficiary category, drug type (generic or brand), and the pharmacy option used.

Veterans may qualify for VA dental care based on their service history and disability status, with eligibility divided into benefit classes. Free dental care is available to veterans with service-connected dental conditions, former prisoners of war, veterans rated at 100% disability, and certain other groups including those participating in vocational rehabilitation or receiving homeless services. Veterans not eligible for free care may enroll in the VA Dental Insurance Program (VADIP), which offers reduced-cost dental coverage to those enrolled in VA health care or CHAMPVA.

The Exceptional Family Member Program (EFMP) is a mandatory enrollment program for active-duty service members who have a dependent — child, spouse, or adult — with special medical or educational needs requiring specialized care or services. Enrollment ensures that the dependent's needs are considered when assignment and PCS orders are issued, so the family is not placed where required services are unavailable. Support includes special needs consultations, respite care, educational assistance coordination, and EFMP family support coordinators at no cost through Military OneSource.

The Military Family Life Counseling (MFLC) program provides free, confidential counseling by licensed professionals to service members and their immediate family members for issues such as relationship stress, parenting challenges, grief, and deployment adjustments. Sessions are available in-person, by phone, or via video on and off military installations with no referral required and no records kept — keeping it separate from clinical mental health care. To find a counselor, contact your installation's Family Support Center or call Military OneSource at 800-342-9647.

Military OneSource is a free, 24/7 DoD program providing information, referrals, and direct services to active-duty, National Guard, and Reserve service members and their families worldwide. Services include confidential non-medical counseling, free tax preparation (MilTax), financial coaching, relocation assistance, spouse employment support, and emergency resources. Available online at MilitaryOneSource.mil, by phone at 800-342-9647, and through the Military OneSource app.

Military families have access to subsidized child care through on-installation Child Development Centers (CDCs) for infants through preschool, Family Child Care (FCC) homes for flexible scheduling, and School-Age Care (SAC) programs for children through sixth grade, with fees based on total family income. For families who cannot access on-installation care, the Military Child Care in Your Neighborhood (MCCYN) program provides fee assistance at community child care centers and family child care homes. Active-duty, National Guard, and Reserve families can search for and manage care through MilitaryChildCare.com.

Service members who complete at least 20 years of qualifying active duty service earn a monthly retirement annuity for life, with the amount determined by their retirement system. The legacy High-3 system (for those who entered before January 1, 2018) pays 2.5% of the highest 36-month average basic pay per year of service — 50% at 20 years and up to 100% at 40 years — while the Blended Retirement System (BRS, for those entering on or after January 1, 2018) uses a 2.0% multiplier. Both systems include annual Cost-of-Living Adjustments; BRS also provides government TSP contributions during the service career.

Veterans Service Organizations (VSOs) are VA-recognized groups whose accredited representatives provide free assistance to veterans navigating VA benefits, disability claims, and appeals. Their services include gathering evidence, filing claims, requesting decision reviews, and communicating directly with the VA on a veteran's behalf — at no cost. Veterans can find and appoint an accredited VSO representative using the VA's online locator at va.gov/get-help-from-accredited-representative/find-rep/.

Veterans' Preference gives eligible veterans a scoring advantage in competitive federal hiring: 5 points are added for honorable service during qualifying periods or campaigns, and 10 points are added for veterans with any service-connected disability (including ratings below 10%), Purple Heart recipients, and certain surviving family members of veterans. The preference points are added to numerical examination scores, and agencies must provide documented justification to pass over a preference-eligible applicant. Veterans' preference applies to most new competitive civil service appointments but does not cover internal promotions, transfers, or reassignments.

Combat-Related Special Compensation (CRSC) provides tax-free monthly payments to military retirees with combat-related disabilities, restoring some or all of the retired pay that was offset by VA disability compensation. To qualify, retirees must have military retirement pay and a VA disability rating of at least 10% directly linked to armed conflict, hazardous service, simulated war training, or an instrumentality of war — or a disability connected to a Purple Heart. Applications are submitted via DD Form 2860 to the retiree's branch of service; claims must be filed within six years to receive full retroactive payments.

Concurrent Retirement and Disability Pay (CRDP) allows eligible military retirees with a VA disability rating of 50% or higher to receive both their full military retired pay and VA disability compensation simultaneously, without the traditional dollar-for-dollar offset. Enrollment is automatic — DFAS audits accounts and may issue retroactive payments going back up to six years from the date of eligibility. Retirees who qualify for both CRSC and CRDP must elect one or the other; they cannot receive both programs at the same time.

konular.baslik: FSGLI — Family Servicemembers' Group Life Insurance
konular.ozet: Family Servicemembers' Group Life Insurance (FSGLI) provides automatic term life coverage of up to $100,000 — but not exceeding the service member's own SGLI amount — for spouses of SGLI-covered service members, and $10,000 at no cost for each dependent child until age 18 (extending to age 22 for full-time college students, or indefinitely for children who became disabled before age 18). Spouses married after January 2, 2013 must enroll through the SGLI Online Enrollment System via milConnect; those married before that date were automatically enrolled. Monthly premiums vary by the spouse's age.

The My Career Advancement Account (MyCAA) Scholarship provides eligible military spouses with up to $4,000 in financial assistance (up to $2,000 per fiscal year) to pursue portable career credentials, including licenses, certifications, associate degrees, or continuing education courses at approved institutions. Eligibility requires the military sponsor to be on active Title 10 orders in pay grades E-1 through O-3, or Warrant Officers W-1 and W-2, including activated National Guard and Reserve members in those grades. Spouses apply through the MyCAA program website, and free 24/7 career coaching is available through Military OneSource at 800-342-9647.

Veterans' Group Life Insurance (VGLI) allows service members to convert their SGLI coverage to a renewable civilian term life insurance policy after separation, in an amount matching their final SGLI coverage and ranging from $10,000 to $500,000. Applications must be submitted within 1 year and 120 days of separation; applying within the first 240 days requires no proof of good health, while later applications require health verification. Coverage may be increased by $25,000 every five years up to the program maximum until age 60.

The VA Interest Rate Reduction Refinance Loan (IRRRL), commonly called a VA streamline refinance, allows veterans with an existing VA-backed home loan to refinance at a lower interest rate or to convert from an adjustable-rate to a fixed-rate mortgage — in most cases without a new appraisal or full credit underwriting. The VA funding fee and closing costs can be rolled into the new loan balance, making it possible to refinance with little to no upfront expense. To apply, contact any VA-approved private lender.

konular.baslik: VA Aid and Attendance / Housebound Benefits
konular.ozet: Aid and Attendance is a supplemental monthly pension payment for veterans or surviving spouses who require help with daily activities such as bathing, dressing, or eating; are bedridden; live in a nursing home due to mental or physical incapacity; or have severely limited vision. Housebound is a similar benefit for veterans substantially confined to their home due to a permanent disability — a beneficiary may receive one or the other, but not both simultaneously. Applications are submitted online at VA.gov, by mail using VA Form 21-2680 to the Pension Intake Center in Janesville, WI, or in person at the nearest VA regional office.

The Transition Assistance Program (TAP) is a mandatory DoD, VA, and Department of Labor program that prepares separating and retiring service members for civilian life, covering VA benefits, employment, education, financial management, and entrepreneurship. Service members with at least 180 continuous days on active duty begin TAP one year before separation or two years before retirement; spouses are eligible for certain components. The VA Benefits and Services TAP module is also available online at TAPevents.mil.

The Uniformed Services Employment and Reemployment Rights Act (USERRA) requires all public and private employers to promptly reemploy service members returning from military service in the same or a comparable position with the same seniority, pay, and benefits — provided cumulative military absences do not exceed five years. USERRA also prohibits discrimination in hiring, promotion, or other employment actions against anyone based on past, present, or future military service. Employees who believe their USERRA rights were violated can file a complaint with the Department of Labor's Veterans' Employment and Training Service (VETS) at dol.gov/vets.

The Overseas Housing Allowance (OHA) is a reimbursement-based program that enables service members assigned to permanent duty overseas to privately lease civilian housing on the local economy when on-base quarters are unavailable or not assigned. Unlike the domestic BAH fixed-payment model, OHA reimburses the actual amounts service members spend on rent, utilities, and associated move-in costs, up to established rate ceilings that vary by pay grade and duty location. Service members initiate OHA by submitting DD Form 2367 with their lease agreement through their installation's housing or finance office.

The Dislocation Allowance (DLA) partially reimburses service members for household relocation expenses incurred during a Permanent Change of Station (PCS) move, evacuation, or government-ordered housing change; it is generally authorized once per fiscal year. DLA is not available for a first PCS without dependents, for active duty entry without dependents, or for separation and retirement travel. Two rates apply — with or without dependents — based on whether authorized dependents also relocated; current rates are published by the Defense Travel Management Office and reimbursement is filed at the installation finance office.

TRICARE Select is a self-managed preferred provider organization (PPO) health plan available in the U.S. that allows eligible beneficiaries to see any TRICARE-authorized provider without a referral in most cases. Eligible beneficiaries include active-duty family members, retired service members and their families, activated National Guard and Reserve families, retirees age 60 and older, survivors, Medal of Honor recipients, and qualified former spouses — active-duty service members themselves are not eligible for this plan. Enrollment is required and costs include an annual enrollment fee, an outpatient deductible, and copayments that vary based on the military sponsor's status.

VA Vet Centers are community-based counseling centers that provide individual, group, marriage, and family counseling — including treatment for PTSD, depression, and military sexual trauma — at no cost to veterans, active-duty service members, National Guard, Reserve members, and their family members, in a non-clinical setting separate from the VA medical system. No VA enrollment or benefits eligibility is required to access Vet Center services. Reach the confidential 24/7 Vet Center Call Center at 1-877-927-8387 or find the nearest location using the VA.gov locator.

Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) is a tax-free monthly benefit paid to the surviving spouse, children, and parents of a service member or veteran who died on active duty or from a service-connected illness or injury — or who had a totally disabling service-connected rating for a specified period before death. Eligible surviving spouses must have been married to the veteran for at least one year, or have a child together, or have married within 15 years of the veteran's discharge. Apply using VA Form 21P-534EZ (spouse or child) or 21P-535 (parent) online at VA.gov, by mail to the VA Pension Intake Center in Janesville, WI, or through an accredited VSO representative.

The VA Survivors Pension provides monthly payments to eligible surviving spouses and unmarried dependent children of deceased wartime veterans whose income and net worth fall below limits set annually by Congress. Surviving spouses must not have remarried after the veteran's death, and the veteran must have served at least one day during a recognized wartime period without a dishonorable discharge; enlistees after September 7, 1980 generally require at least 24 months of active duty including wartime service. Applications are submitted online at VA.gov, by mail using VA Form 21P-534EZ to the Pension Intake Center in Janesville, WI, or in person at a VA regional office.

The Montgomery GI Bill Active Duty (MGIB-AD, Chapter 30) provides education benefits for eligible veterans who contributed $1,200 during their service; benefits cover up to 36 months of payments for degree programs, vocational training, flight training, apprenticeships, licensing and certification exams, and online learning. Eligibility requires an honorable discharge, at least a high school diploma or GED, and continuous active duty service — typically two to four years depending on the enlistment agreement. Apply using VA Form 22-1990 at VA.gov and use the GI Bill Comparison Tool to find participating schools.

Military Tuition Assistance (TA) is a DoD program that covers civilian education expenses for eligible active-duty service members — including National Guard and Reserve members on qualifying orders — to pursue degrees, certifications, and coursework while serving, with a government-wide annual cap of $4,500 and up to 18 semester hours per year. Participation requires supervisor or commander approval prior to enrollment; the approval and application process is managed through each branch's education portal (the Army uses ArmyIgnitED; other branches have equivalent platforms). TA can be combined with the GI Bill for the same period of service to help offset remaining education costs.

Veterans who disagree with a VA benefits decision may choose one of three review paths: a Supplemental Claim (submit new and relevant evidence), a Higher-Level Review (a more senior reviewer re-examines the case without new evidence), or an appeal to the Board of Veterans' Appeals before a Veterans Law Judge. Veterans may switch between review lanes as their case develops, and each path has different processing timelines. Accredited VSO representatives assist at no cost; attorneys and claims agents may charge agreed-upon fees — reach the VA at 800-827-1000 or use VA.gov to start a review.

Hazardous Duty Incentive Pay (HDIP) is monthly special compensation for service members assigned to perform duties officially designated as hazardous by the Secretary of Defense, including flight duty, parachute jumping, demolition of explosives, and handling of toxic fuels or propellants. Payment amounts and eligibility vary by the specific type of hazardous duty and the member's component and status; rates are set by DoD and updated periodically. Contact your unit's finance or personnel office to confirm eligibility and initiate payment.

Temporary Lodging Allowance (TLA) partially reimburses service members and their families for higher-than-normal lodging expenses incurred when assigned to temporary quarters outside the continental U.S. (OCONUS) during a PCS move. Eligible beneficiaries include active-duty service members, National Guard and Reserve members on qualifying orders, transitioning service members, and their spouses. Contact your installation's local pay office — searchable through MilitaryINSTALLATIONS at militaryonesource.mil — for current rates and to initiate payment.

DoD SkillBridge allows transitioning service members to gain civilian work experience through industry training, apprenticeships, or internships during their last 180 days of service, while continuing to receive full military pay and benefits — at no cost to the employer. Participation requires written authorization from a field grade commander; once approved, service members may spend up to 180 days training full-time with approved industry providers who can evaluate them for future employment. SkillBridge bridges the gap between military service and civilian employment by offering real-world, in-demand job experience before separation.

Defense Commissary Agency (DeCA) stores offer tax-free grocery and household shopping at reduced prices to active-duty service members, Guard and Reserve members, military retirees, veterans with any VA service-connected disability rating, Medal of Honor and Purple Heart recipients, former POWs, primary family caregivers enrolled in the VA's PCAFC program, and their authorized family members. Veterans and caregivers must present a current Veteran Health Identification Card (VHIC) or other VA-issued eligibility documentation, and must enroll for recurring installation access at the local visitor center (includes identity verification and background screening). Commissary privileges were expanded to service-connected disabled veterans effective January 1, 2020.

TRICARE Prime is a managed care plan that assigns each enrolled beneficiary a Primary Care Manager (PCM) — at a military treatment facility or civilian network provider — who coordinates all routine care and specialist referrals; active-duty service members and their families pay no out-of-pocket costs, while retirees and other beneficiaries pay annual enrollment fees and copayments. The plan is only available in designated Prime Service Areas, so beneficiaries outside those areas should consider TRICARE Prime Remote or TRICARE Select instead. Enrollment is required and can be completed through the TRICARE Plan Finder tool at tricare.mil.

VA telehealth allows enrolled veterans to consult with VA health care providers by video from home or from a community ATLAS (Accessing Telehealth through Local Area Stations) location, using the free VA Video Connect app on any internet-connected device — smartphone, tablet, or computer. Standard telehealth visits carry no copay, and the VA can provide an iPad to eligible veterans who lack a video-capable device or internet access at home. Ask your VA care team to set up a telehealth appointment, or visit telehealth.va.gov for more information.

VET TEC 2.0 (Veterans Employment Through Technology Education Courses) pays tuition and fees directly to approved training providers for veterans pursuing high-demand technology careers — including computer programming, software development, data processing, and information sciences — plus a monthly housing allowance equal to the Post-9/11 GI Bill rate and a books and supplies stipend. Eligibility requires an honorable or other-than-dishonorable discharge (or active-duty status within 180 days of separation), at least 36 months of active duty service, and being under age 62; up to 4,000 participants are accepted per fiscal year and the program is funded through September 30, 2027. Apply through VA.gov and use the GI Bill Comparison Tool to find approved high-tech training providers.

Under federal law (Public Law 106-65), any eligible veteran or active-duty service member is entitled to a military funeral honors ceremony upon family request at no cost, consisting of at minimum the playing of Taps and the folding and presentation of the American flag to the next of kin. Eligibility requires an honorable or general (under honorable conditions) discharge from active military service, or qualifying service in the Selected Reserve; veterans with dishonorable or bad-conduct discharges are not eligible. Families request the ceremony by providing the veteran's DD-214 or other discharge documentation to the funeral home, which coordinates directly with the appropriate military branch.

Space Available (Space-A) travel allows eligible military personnel and their families to fly on U.S. military aircraft at no cost when seats are available after mission passengers are accommodated, with six priority categories (Cat I–VI) determining boarding order — active-duty members on emergency or ordinary leave board before retirees and Reserve members. Passengers register by submitting AMC Form 140 online or in person at a military passenger terminal up to 60 days in advance; within each priority category, seats are assigned in first-in, first-out order by sign-up date. Valid military ID and all required travel documents (including a passport for international flights) are required.

Army & Air Force Exchange Service (AAFES) exchanges — known as BX, PX, or PXtra — are open to active-duty service members and their families, military retirees, 100% disabled veterans, Medal of Honor recipients, and foreign military members on duty with U.S. forces. Honorably discharged veterans (including general under honorable conditions discharges) have online shopping access at shopmyexchange.com after verifying eligibility through VetVerify.org — but online access excludes alcohol, tobacco, and military uniforms, and does not grant physical store entry. Valid military ID or dependent ID is required for in-store purchases.

Selected Reserve and National Guard members who commit to a 6-year service obligation may receive up to 36 months of education and training benefits under MGIB-SR (Chapter 1606). To apply, obtain a Notice of Basic Eligibility (DD Form 2384-1) from your unit, then submit VA Form 22-1990; you must maintain good standing in an active Selected Reserve unit to retain eligibility. Members discharged due to a service-connected disability not caused by misconduct remain eligible.

Women Veterans enrolled in VA health care receive comprehensive services including primary care, cancer screenings (breast, cervical, colon, lung), reproductive health, mental health care, and free confidential treatment for military sexual trauma (MST). Women Veterans may access MST-related care even without a disability rating or minimum service length requirement. The Women Veterans Call Center is available at 855-829-6636.

Active duty members and command-sponsored families stationed overseas use TRICARE Prime Overseas (managed care with no enrollment fees or copays for authorized care); retirees and their families use TRICARE Select Overseas (flexible, pay upfront then file claims for reimbursement). Both plans are administered by International SOS and require DEERS enrollment; TRICARE Prime Overseas is not available to retirees. Military ID serves as proof of coverage in place of a wallet card.

MilTax is a free DoD tax preparation service available through Military OneSource, providing specialized software and one-on-one consultations with tax professionals trained in military-specific issues such as PCS moves, combat pay exclusions, and military spouse residency rules. Eligible users can file a federal return and up to five state returns at no cost, with a 100% accuracy guarantee and maximum refund guarantee. Available to active-duty members, eligible family members, survivors, and veterans up to 365 days after separation or retirement; access begins at MilitaryOneSource.mil or by calling 800-342-9647.

The VA provides free, confidential health care for physical and mental health conditions related to military sexual trauma (MST) — defined as sexual assault or threatening sexual harassment experienced during military service. Eligibility is broad: veterans with Other Than Honorable discharges, those with less than two years of service, current service members, and National Guard and Reserve members with federal active duty may qualify; no disability rating or prior reporting of the incident is required. Every VA medical facility has a designated MST coordinator; support is also available through Vet Centers, by calling 988 and pressing 1 (Veterans Crisis Line), or by texting 838255.

The Uniformed Services Former Spouses' Protection Act (USFSPA) authorizes state courts to treat military retired pay as divisible marital property and award a portion to a former spouse; it also provides a mechanism for enforcing court-ordered child support and alimony against military pay. A former spouse must first obtain a qualifying court order specifically awarding them a share of the retired pay before the Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS) can issue direct payments. For application procedures and USFSPA FAQs, contact DFAS through the AskDFAS system at DFAS.mil.

Veterans and their next of kin can request a DD-214 or other military service records online through the eVetRecs system at the National Archives, requiring identity verification through ID.me; Standard Form 180 may also be submitted by mail or fax to the National Personnel Records Center (NPRC) in St. Louis, MO — email requests are not accepted. Most basic requests are free; processing typically takes 90 or more days due to high request volume, and status can be checked by calling 314-801-0800 on weekdays. Next of kin eligible to request includes surviving spouses, parents, children, and siblings of deceased veterans.

Under the MISSION Act, VA Community Care allows eligible veterans to receive health care from non-VA providers in the community when VA cannot provide the needed care — such as when an appointment is not available within access standards or the required service is not offered at a VA facility. Veterans must obtain VA authorization before seeking community care; emergency care is an exception. Covered services include general care, urgent care, home health, hospice, and in vitro fertilization for eligible veterans; copayments may apply for non-service-connected conditions.

konular.baslik: Blue Water Navy — Agent Orange Benefits
konular.ozet: The Blue Water Navy Vietnam Veterans Act of 2019 extended Agent Orange presumptive exposure to veterans who served on U.S. military vessels operating within 12 nautical miles of the Vietnamese and Cambodian coastline between January 9, 1962, and May 7, 1975. Eligible veterans may receive VA disability compensation for a wide range of presumptive conditions including certain cancers (bladder, prostate, non-Hodgkin's lymphoma), Type 2 diabetes, heart disease, Parkinson's disease, and high blood pressure, among others. Veterans whose prior claims were denied may file a Supplemental Claim for reconsideration with potential retroactive payments to the original claim date.

Temporary Lodging Expense (TLE) partially reimburses service members for lodging and meal costs while staying in temporary quarters during a Permanent Change of Station (PCS) move within the continental United States (CONUS). TLE may be paid for any day that per diem for PCS travel is not paid — both benefits cannot be claimed on the same day. To initiate TLE, contact your installation's local pay office; full rate details and FAQs are available at travel.dod.mil.

The VA's Veterans Justice Outreach (VJO) program places specialists at VA medical centers to identify veterans involved in the criminal justice system — through courts, jails, and prisons — and connect them with VA mental health treatment, substance abuse services, housing assistance, and case management as an alternative to or in parallel with incarceration. VJO works closely with Veterans Treatment Courts, which provide specialized judicial oversight and treatment-focused alternatives for eligible veterans. Contact your local VA medical center's VJO specialist, or call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838 (24/7).

Coast Guard Mutual Assistance (CGMA) provides interest-free loans, grants, and financial counseling to active-duty and reserve Coast Guard members, Auxiliary members, retirees, civilian employees, PHS officers, chaplains, and surviving spouses facing financial hardship. Assistance is organized into emergency aid, day-to-day support, and education programs — including the Supplemental Education Grant covering fees, books, and study materials — plus specialized grants for adoption and new families. Find your local representative through the mycgma.org locator or call 800-881-2462; CGMA is 100% funded by donations.

The Navy-Marine Corps Relief Society (NMCRS) provides interest-free loans and grants to active-duty Sailors, Marines, and their families facing financial hardship, covering needs such as emergency travel, food, rent, utilities, medical expenses, and education assistance. The Society also offers disaster relief support and connects clients with additional community resources. Find the nearest NMCRS location through the nmcrs.org office locator.

The Uniformed and Overseas Citizens Absentee Voting Act (UOCAVA) is the federal law that guarantees the right to register and vote absentee in federal elections for active-duty military members, the Merchant Marine, commissioned USPHS and NOAA officers, and their eligible family members overseas. The MOVE Act further requires states to send absentee ballots at least 45 days before a federal election. Service members and family members can register, request a ballot, and track submissions for free through the Federal Voting Assistance Program at fvap.gov.

A Presidential Memorial Certificate is an engraved paper certificate signed by the incumbent president, honoring the military service of a deceased veteran or reservist who qualifies for national cemetery burial. The requestor must be the veteran's next of kin, a family member, a close friend, or an authorized representative. Requests can be submitted at no cost online at VA.gov, by mail, in person at a VA regional office, or by fax using VA Form 40-0247.

The PACT Act (2022) expands VA health care and disability compensation to veterans exposed to burn pits, Agent Orange, and other toxic substances across Vietnam War, Gulf War, and post-9/11 eras of service. The law adds over 20 presumptive conditions — including various cancers and respiratory illnesses — that are automatically service-connected, so veterans no longer need to prove a direct link between their condition and military service. Veterans in covered service eras can enroll in VA health care and file disability claims at VA.gov without first needing an existing service-connected disability rating.

Veterans who served in Vietnam, Thailand, Laos, Cambodia, Guam, American Samoa, Johnston Atoll, or near the Korean DMZ during specified periods may qualify for VA disability compensation if diagnosed with a presumptive condition linked to Agent Orange exposure. Presumptive conditions include certain cancers, Type 2 diabetes, heart disease, Parkinson's disease, and hypertension — no direct proof of herbicide contact is required when qualifying service is documented. File a claim online at VA.gov; veterans with previously denied claims may file a Supplemental Claim for reconsideration.

The Post-9/11 GI Bill provides education benefits to veterans who served on active duty after September 10, 2001, covering tuition and fees, a monthly housing allowance, and a books and supplies stipend for up to 36 months (or 48 months combined with other service periods). Eligibility requires at least 90 days of active duty after 9/11, a service-connected disability discharge after 30 continuous days, or receipt of a Purple Heart; the Yellow Ribbon Program supplements coverage for 100%-eligible beneficiaries attending private or graduate institutions. Apply using VA Form 22-1990 at VA.gov and use the GI Bill Comparison Tool to find approved schools.

konular.baslik: TRICARE ECHO — Extended Care Health Option
konular.ozet: ECHO (Extended Care Health Option) is a TRICARE supplemental program providing additional services and supplies to eligible beneficiaries with special needs — including autism spectrum disorder, serious physical disabilities, and complex homebound conditions — who are enrolled in the Exceptional Family Member Program (EFMP). Beneficiaries must also be enrolled in TRICARE Prime or TRICARE Select, and all ECHO services require pre-authorization from the regional TRICARE contractor. Contact your TRICARE regional contractor or visit tricare.mil/Plans/SpecialPrograms/ECHO for coverage details and enrollment.

konular.baslik: VA Certificate of Eligibility (COE) for Home Loans
konular.ozet: A VA Certificate of Eligibility (COE) verifies to a lender that a borrower qualifies for a VA-backed home loan; veterans, active-duty service members, National Guard and Reserve members, and eligible surviving spouses may all apply. Veterans typically need their DD Form 214, while active-duty members need a current statement of service; applications can be submitted through eBenefits, directly through a VA-approved lender's Web LGY system, or by mail using VA Form 26-1880. Obtaining a COE is the first required step toward using the VA home loan benefit for purchase, construction, or refinancing.

The Family Advocacy Program (FAP) is a DoD program that provides prevention, intervention, and support services for military families affected by domestic abuse and child abuse or neglect, working in coordination with military and civilian agencies. The program connects victims with advocacy services, safety planning, and counseling, while also supporting service members and their partners in building healthy relationships. FAP services are available at military installations through the installation's Family Support Center; Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 can help locate the nearest FAP office.

konular.baslik: How to File a VA Disability Claim
konular.ozet: Veterans can file a VA disability claim online at VA.gov, by mail, in person at a VA regional office, by fax, or through an accredited VSO representative; gathering evidence beforehand — including VA and private medical records and buddy statements — can expedite processing. Evidence should document the current condition, a diagnosis, and a link between the condition and military service (or show it existed before service and was aggravated by it). After filing, the VA may schedule a Compensation and Pension (C&P) exam to evaluate the claimed disability before issuing a rating decision.

The Veterans Crisis Line offers free, confidential 24/7 support to veterans, service members, and their loved ones in emotional distress or suicidal crisis — no VA enrollment or service history is required to call. Reach a trained responder by calling 988 and pressing 1, texting 838255, or chatting online at veteranscrisisline.net. Responders are available around the clock to provide immediate support and, when needed, connect callers with local mental health resources.

Eligibility for a VA-backed home loan depends on service history: active-duty members generally need at least 90 continuous days, veterans must meet era-specific requirements (typically 24 continuous months or the full period called up, though some periods require only 90–181 days), and eligible surviving spouses of veterans who died in service or from a service-connected disability may also qualify. In all cases, service must have ended under conditions other than dishonorable. After confirming eligibility, borrowers must obtain a Certificate of Eligibility (COE) before applying for a VA-backed loan through a private lender.

The Servicemembers Civil Relief Act (SCRA) protects active-duty service members, National Guard, and Reserve members called to federal active duty by capping interest rates on pre-service debts at 6%, blocking landlords from evicting service members without a court order for non-payment of rent, and allowing service members to terminate residential leases and auto leases upon receiving PCS orders or a deployment directive. The SCRA also protects against foreclosure, vehicle repossession, and default judgments while a service member is deployed. Service members can assert SCRA rights by providing written notice and a copy of their orders to the creditor or landlord.

The Survivor Benefit Plan (SBP) is a DoD program that provides monthly, cost-of-living-adjusted income to the eligible survivors of deceased service members and retirees. Active-duty service members are automatically covered at no cost; retiring service members must elect SBP coverage at the time of retirement, with premiums deducted from retired pay. Retirees who are also eligible for VA Dependency and Indemnity Compensation (DIC) may receive a Special Survivors Indemnity Allowance (SSIA) to offset reductions.

The Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers (PCAFC) provides support to family members caring for veterans with serious service-connected disabilities, including a financial stipend, access to health insurance, mental health counseling, caregiver training, and respite care. To be eligible, the veteran must have a service-connected disability and require personal care services due to that condition. Apply through VA.gov or contact your local VA facility's caregiver support coordinator.

A VA Compensation and Pension (C&P) exam is a medical evaluation the VA may schedule after a veteran files a disability claim to assess whether a condition exists and to determine its severity for rating purposes. The examiner reviews medical records, conducts a physical examination, and may order additional tests — the purpose is information gathering, not treatment. Veterans should attend the exam if scheduled; missing it without rescheduling can result in a claim denial.

VA Vocational Rehabilitation and Employment (VR&E, Chapter 31) helps veterans and service members with service-connected disabilities that limit their ability to work by funding education, job training, employment accommodations, resume development, and job-seeking assistance — in some cases, certain family members may also access services. Eligible participants work with a VA vocational rehabilitation counselor to develop an individualized rehabilitation plan. Veterans can start their application online at VA.gov under the Careers and Employment section.

Veterans Affairs Life Insurance (VALife) is a low-cost whole-life insurance program available to veterans who have any VA service-connected disability rating; veterans age 80 or younger with any service-connected rating are eligible, while those over 80 face stricter requirements. Coverage is available in amounts from $10,000 to $40,000 in $10,000 increments and does not require a medical exam for qualified applicants. Apply online at VA.gov under Life Insurance.

The VA rates service-connected disabilities on a scale of 0–100% in 10% increments, representing how much the condition reduces a veteran's overall health and ability to function. When a veteran has multiple disabilities, the VA applies the 'whole person' combined ratings formula — ranked from highest to lowest and cross-referenced on a combined ratings table — which ensures the total never exceeds 100%; the result is rounded to the nearest 10%. The combined rating determines the monthly tax-free compensation amount and eligibility for certain additional benefits.

The VA Pension is a monthly tax-free benefit for wartime veterans who meet age or disability requirements and whose income and net worth fall within limits set by Congress — service must not have ended in a dishonorable discharge and must include at least one day during a recognized wartime period. Veterans age 65 or older may qualify regardless of disability status; veterans under 65 must be permanently and totally disabled. Applications can be submitted online at VA.gov, by mail, in person at a VA regional office, or through an accredited VSO representative.

konular.baslik: In-State Tuition for Veterans (Veterans Choice Act §702)
konular.ozet: Section 702 of the Veterans Choice Act requires public colleges and universities with VA-approved programs to charge in-state tuition rates to eligible veterans, service members, and their dependents regardless of residency status. Covered individuals must have served 90 or more days on active duty since September 11, 2001; schools that refuse in-state rates to qualifying beneficiaries risk losing VA education funding. To confirm a school's compliance, veterans can use the VA's GI Bill Comparison Tool at va.gov/education/gi-bill-comparison-tool.

The Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) is a federal government-sponsored retirement savings and investment plan offering tax-advantaged benefits similar to a private 401(k), available to all service members and federal civilian employees. Service members covered by the Blended Retirement System (BRS) who entered service on or after January 1, 2018, are automatically enrolled at a 3% basic-pay contribution rate after 60 days of service; contributions can be adjusted or stopped at any time. TSP offers traditional (pre-tax) and Roth (post-tax) options and a selection of diversified investment funds.

Military legal assistance offices — staffed by Judge Advocate General (JAG) attorneys — provide free legal services to active-duty service members and their families on matters such as estate planning, wills, powers of attorney, adoption, lease agreements, and family law. Availability and specific services vary by installation and branch; many offices require an appointment. Call Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 or visit your nearest installation's legal office to find available services.

TRICARE provides dental benefits based on military status: active-duty service members receive dental coverage through active-duty dental benefits at no cost, while eligible family members and retirees may enroll in the TRICARE Dental Program (TDP) or the Federal Employees Dental and Vision Insurance Program (FEDVIP), depending on their eligibility. Coverage details, plan comparisons, and enrollment information are available at tricare.mil.

The Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA) program, also known as Chapter 35, provides monthly education payments to eligible spouses and children of veterans or service members who have died, are captured or missing in action, or have a permanent and total service-connected disability. Benefits can be applied toward undergraduate or graduate degrees, vocational training, apprenticeships, and other approved educational programs for up to 36 to 45 months depending on eligibility tier. Apply using VA Form 22-5490 at VA.gov.

TRICARE covers eye exams and services to diagnose or treat medical conditions of the eye for eligible beneficiaries; active-duty family members receive coverage for annual routine eye exams regardless of plan, while retirees enrolled in TRICARE Prime receive coverage every two years. Eligible beneficiaries may also access additional vision insurance — including eyeglasses and contact lenses — through the Federal Employees Dental and Vision Insurance Program (FEDVIP). Coverage details and FEDVIP enrollment are available at tricare.mil.

The VA operates several programs to prevent and end veteran homelessness: HUD-VASH pairs HUD housing vouchers with VA case management for permanent supportive housing; the Supportive Services for Veteran Families (SSVF) program offers prevention assistance and rapid re-housing; the Grant and Per Diem program funds transitional housing through community organizations; and Health Care for Homeless Veterans provides outreach and clinical treatment. Veterans experiencing or at risk of homelessness can call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838 (available 24/7) or visit VA.gov to find local resources.

TRICARE For Life (TFL) is Medicare-wraparound coverage available to TRICARE-eligible military retirees and their family members who hold both Medicare Part A and Part B, regardless of age or where they live. TFL acts as a secondary payer after Medicare, covering most Medicare cost-sharing so that beneficiaries typically have little or no out-of-pocket expense for covered services. Enrollment is automatic once a beneficiary obtains Medicare Part A and Part B — no separate application is required and there are no TFL enrollment fees.

The VA provides a burial allowance to help cover funeral and burial costs for eligible veterans who did not receive a dishonorable discharge and died under qualifying circumstances; as of October 1, 2025, the maximum allowance is $1,002 for burial costs plus an additional $1,002 for plot or interment expenses. Eligible applicants include the surviving spouse, children, parents, executor of the estate, or the funeral home — they must apply within two years of the veteran's burial. Submit VA Form 21P-530EZ online at VA.gov or by mail.

The VA offers dental care through multiple eligibility classes based on service history and health status: veterans with service-connected dental disabilities, those rated 100% disabled (permanent and total), former prisoners of war, and veterans experiencing homelessness may qualify for comprehensive dental treatment. Veterans who do not meet an eligibility class for direct VA dental care can purchase reduced-cost dental coverage through the VA Dental Insurance Program (VADIP). Check your eligibility and find a VA dental clinic at VA.gov under Health Care.

The Exceptional Family Member Program (EFMP) supports military families with a member — whether a spouse, child, or dependent adult — who has special medical or educational needs, providing access to specialized consultations, caregiver support, counseling, and other family support services. EFMP enrollment is a critical step for PCS moves, ensuring that the receiving installation has the medical and educational resources required before orders are issued. Contact your installation's EFMP coordinator or call Military OneSource at 800-342-9647 for enrollment assistance.

Veterans diagnosed with PTSD related to a traumatic event during military service may qualify for tax-free monthly VA disability compensation. To file a PTSD claim, veterans submit a disability application along with VA Form 21-0781 documenting the in-service traumatic event that caused or contributed to the condition. Approved veterans become eligible for both monthly disability payments and VA health care benefits for PTSD treatment.

konular.baslik: Burial in a VA National Cemetery
konular.ozet: Eligible veterans, active-duty service members, and certain family members may be buried in a VA national cemetery at no cost — eligible veterans include those discharged under honorable conditions, with enlisted service after September 7, 1980 or officer service after October 16, 1981 generally requiring 24 continuous months of active duty. Eligible dependents (spouses and dependent children) of qualifying veterans may also be interred in a national cemetery. To request burial, the next of kin contacts the National Cemetery Scheduling Office at 800-535-1117.

Family Separation Allowance (FSA) is additional monthly pay provided to service members — including National Guard and Reserve members — whose family members are unable to live with or near them at their permanent duty station due to military requirements. FSA applies in situations both within the U.S. and overseas where families cannot reside together, helping offset the added costs of maintaining separate households. Contact your installation's finance office to initiate or verify FSA entitlement.

konular.baslik: Marine Gunnery Sergeant John David Fry Scholarship
konular.ozet: The Fry Scholarship provides Post-9/11 GI Bill-equivalent education benefits — covering tuition, a monthly housing allowance, and books and supplies stipend — to the children and surviving spouses of service members or Selected Reserve members who died in the line of duty on or after September 11, 2001. Benefits are available for up to 36 months; eligibility timelines vary depending on the date of the service member's death, with some beneficiaries having no expiration deadline. Apply using VA Form 22-5490 at VA.gov.

VA Chapter 36 — Personalized Career Planning and Guidance — is a free program offering educational and career counseling, planning, and resources to eligible veterans and their dependents who qualify for VA education benefits. Services include career aptitude assessments, resume support, education planning, and academic guidance to help with post-military employment transitions. There is no cost; apply at VA.gov under the Careers and Employment section.

The VA offers two housing grants for disabled veterans: the Specially Adapted Housing (SAH) grant (up to $126,526 for FY 2026) for veterans with the loss or loss of use of more than one limb or comparable qualifying disabilities, and the Special Home Adaptation (SHA) grant (up to $25,350 for FY 2026) for those with the loss of both hands or severe burns. Both grants can be used to buy, build, or modify a permanent home to increase the veteran's independence and accommodate their disability. Apply at VA.gov under the Housing Assistance section.

Veterans who served on active duty and received an honorable (or other-than-dishonorable) discharge are generally eligible to apply for VA health care; the main eligibility groups include veterans with service-connected disabilities, combat veterans within five years of discharge, veterans exposed to specific hazardous materials, and veterans who qualify based on income. Eligibility is prioritized across eight enrollment categories, with the VA reserving the right to limit enrollment for lower-priority groups based on funding. Veterans can enroll online at VA.gov or call 877-222-8387.

Former prisoners of war (POWs) may qualify for VA disability compensation for a broad range of presumptive conditions linked to captivity — including osteoporosis, hypertensive vascular disease, PTSD, psychosis, and many others — without needing to prove a direct service connection. Former POWs who receive any disability rating also gain access to VA health care enrollment. File a claim at VA.gov; the VA's National POW Registry can assist in documenting captivity status.

TDIU is a VA benefit that allows veterans who cannot work due to a service-connected disability to receive monthly compensation at the 100% disability rate — even if their actual combined rating is lower. To qualify, a veteran generally needs either a single service-connected disability rated at 60% or higher, or multiple disabilities totaling 70% or higher with at least one rated at 40%. Apply at VA.gov by submitting VA Form 21-8940 along with your existing disability claim or as a new claim.

TRICARE Reserve Select (TRS) is a premium-based health plan available worldwide to qualified Selected Reserve members and their families who are not on active duty for more than 30 days, are not eligible for Federal Employees Health Benefits (FEHB), and are not covered under the Transitional Assistance Management Program (TAMP). Members pay monthly premiums, annual deductibles, and cost-shares when using TRICARE-authorized providers, with lower out-of-pocket costs at network providers; no primary care manager assignment is required. Enroll through the TRICARE Plan Finder at tricare.mil.

The Veteran ID Card (VIC) is a digital photo identification card that veterans can use to access discounts at participating restaurants, hotels, stores, and other businesses that offer veteran benefits. Eligibility requires active duty military service ending in an honorable or general (under honorable conditions) discharge. Veterans apply online at VA.gov by submitting discharge papers, a government-issued ID, and a photo meeting specified requirements.

konular.baslik: VA Home Loan Funding Fee & Disability Exemption
konular.ozet: The VA funding fee is a one-time payment required on VA-backed home loans, ranging from 0.5% to 3.3% of the loan amount depending on the loan type, down payment, and whether it is the borrower's first VA loan use — it can be rolled into the loan balance. Veterans who receive VA disability compensation for a service-connected condition are exempt from paying the funding fee, as are surviving spouses of veterans who died in service or from a service-connected disability. Confirm your exemption status through your lender or at VA.gov.

Veterans can download official VA letters proving their eligibility for benefits directly from VA.gov, including a benefit summary and service verification letter, a benefit verification letter, and letters confirming health care, home loan, and civil service preference eligibility. These documents serve as proof of veteran status and benefit entitlement for use in employment applications, housing, education programs, and other purposes — eliminating the need to request letters by mail. Sign in at VA.gov with your DS Logon, MyHealtheVet, or Login.gov credentials to access the letter download tool.

TRICARE Retired Reserve (TRR) is a premium-based worldwide health plan for retired Reserve and National Guard members under age 60 who qualify for non-regular retirement and are not yet eligible for other federal health benefits. It serves those who have completed their Reserve career and retired but are in the gap period before reaching age 60, when standard military retiree TRICARE eligibility typically begins. Enrollment and plan details are available at tricare.mil.

The VA provides free, comprehensive mental health services — including counseling, therapy, medication, and peer support — for conditions such as PTSD, depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, and substance use problems, available to veterans regardless of VA enrollment status or discharge history. Veterans can access same-day services at any VA medical center, call 877-222-8387, or visit a Vet Center; no appointment or prior enrollment is required for urgent mental health needs. Veterans in crisis can also call 988 and press 1, or text 838255.

TRICARE Prime Remote is a managed care option for active-duty service members, activated National Guard and Reserve members, and their families whose home and duty addresses are more than 50 miles or a one-hour drive from a military treatment facility. Like standard TRICARE Prime, there are no out-of-pocket costs when using the assigned primary care manager; travel expense reimbursement may be available for required appointments. Enroll through the TRICARE Plan Finder at tricare.mil.

Servicemembers' Group Life Insurance (SGLI) is a low-cost term life insurance program that automatically enrolls eligible active-duty service members, Ready Reserve members, and others; coverage is available up to $500,000 in $50,000 increments at a rate of 5 cents per $1,000 of coverage per month. Members may reduce their coverage level in $50,000 increments or decline coverage entirely by submitting a form through their personnel office. Service members should also designate beneficiaries through the SGLI Online Enrollment System (SOES) on milConnect.

Veterans who served in Southwest Asia from August 2, 1990 to the present may qualify for VA disability compensation for medically unexplained illnesses or certain infectious diseases — known as Gulf War illness — as presumptive service-connected conditions without requiring proof of a direct link to service. The VA also provides a free Gulf War Registry health exam to identify long-term health effects of Gulf War service. File a claim at VA.gov and request a Gulf War Registry exam through your local VA facility.

konular.baslik: Camp Lejeune Water Contamination — VA Benefits
konular.ozet: Veterans who served at least 30 days of active duty at Camp Lejeune between August 1, 1953 and December 31, 1987, when the water supply was contaminated with industrial solvents and benzene, are eligible for cost-free VA health care for 16 presumptive conditions including several cancers and Parkinson's disease. Family members who resided at Camp Lejeune during the same period may receive reimbursement for out-of-pocket medical expenses related to those conditions. Veterans can enroll by calling 1-877-222-8387; family members should call 1-866-372-1144.

Veterans enrolled in VA health care receive routine eye exams and preventive vision testing at no cost. Eyeglasses and other visual aids are covered for veterans with a compensable service-connected disability, former POW status, or a vision condition resulting from a VA-treated illness or injury. Veterans who are blind or have low vision may access enhanced rehabilitation services through VA's Visual Impairment Services program.
